summary: Merge workflow and branch management are functional for the standard local Stampede flow, but verification found documentation drift, local-only discovery, simplistic conflict resolution, hardcoded base-branch assumptions, and incomplete cleanup semantics.

findings: Merger discovers branches from result JSON files in the run results directory, not by listing git branches directly.
Only result entries with non-empty branch and status=done are considered, and git rev-parse verifies the branch exists locally.
Discovery is local-only; there is no fetch or remote branch handling.
No fallback discovers existing stampede/* branches when result JSON is missing or malformed.
Duplicate branch references are not deduplicated before merge.
Metaswarm commander branches under havoc-swarm/* are not discovered unless standard result JSON is also emitted.
risks: Distributed or CI flows with remote-only branches will be missed.
Crashed workers can leave orphaned branches that never merge.
Duplicate result JSONs can trigger noisy duplicate merge attempts.
Docs disagree on branch naming in places.
recommendations: Add optional git branch --list fallback for stampede/* after JSON discovery.
Document local-only behavior or add an explicit fetch path.

findings: Merge order is determined by number of files changed versus main, sorted ascending (smallest first).
Sort is deterministic enough because ties fall back to branch text ordering from sort.
README and merger-agent instructions mostly match smallest-first/file-count behavior.
AGENTS.md conflicts by describing output size largest-first behavior.
Missing results directory or no mergeable branches cause hard failure; skipped/missing branch entries are warned or ignored.
risks: Operator confusion from docs saying largest-first/output-size while implementation uses smallest-first/file-count.
File count may be a poor proxy for actual conflict risk or output size.
Hardcoded main can mis-order merges in master-default repos.
recommendations: Align AGENTS.md with implementation or change implementation to match docs.
If output size is intended, sort by diff lines/bytes or result size rather than file count.

findings: Conflicts are detected using git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U.
The script checks for conflict markers and attempts auto-resolution for simple marker counts.
Resolution is sed-based marker removal that keeps both sides, not semantic AI resolution.
Branches with complex conflicts are aborted, skipped, and reported.
Merge report records conflict counts and statuses.
risks: Naive marker removal can concatenate incompatible code and commit broken logic.
No post-resolution syntax or marker validation occurs before git add/commit.
Marker variants or diff3 markers may leak through.
Docs imply AI conflict resolution, but shell implementation is mechanical.
recommendations: Add post-resolution grep for all conflict markers before staging.
Run available syntax/tests for changed files after conflict resolution.
Either implement true AI-assisted conflict resolution in the merger agent or update docs to describe sed-based behavior.
Expand marker matching to include common variants.

findings: Combined branch is named stampede/merged-${RUN_ID}.
Script checks out main, falling back to master, pulls fast-forward only, then deletes and recreates the merged branch.
Merges happen into the combined branch with --no-ff and do not automatically merge to main.
Re-running for the same run id force-deletes the prior local merged branch.
The script changes HEAD and does not restore the user's previous branch.
risks: Unconditional git branch -D can discard local work on an existing merged branch for the same run id.
No clean-working-tree guard before checkout/delete operations.
Diff/order logic hardcodes main even when master fallback is used.
recommendations: Add clean-tree checks before branch operations.
Determine BASE_BRANCH once and use it for checkout, pull, and diffs.
Prefer explicit git switch -C semantics or warn before destructive reset.
Optionally restore the previous branch when merge completes.

findings: Task branches are intentionally retained after worker completion and after merge.
stampede.sh teardown removes runtime artifacts and marks state torn_down but does not delete git branches.
The merged branch persists after merge; a prior merged branch is only force-deleted on rerun.
No git worktrees or temporary branches are used.
Commander/metaswarm havoc-swarm/* branches are not cleaned up and are not documented well.
Claimed task files are cleaned up by worker instructions.
risks: stampede/* and havoc-swarm/* branches accumulate over many runs.
README wording around teardown can imply branch cleanup that does not happen.
Force-deleting merged branch on rerun can lose unpushed local merge work.
recommendations: Clarify docs that teardown does not delete git branches, or add an explicit branch-cleanup mode.
Warn when deleting a pre-existing merged branch.
Provide safe manual cleanup commands for retained branches.

cross_cutting_findings: The core local single-repo merge flow is present and usable.
Base branch handling is inconsistent: checkout supports main/master, but diff/order logic uses main.
Documentation disagrees in several places about branch naming, merge ordering, and cleanup semantics.
Conflict handling is the highest-risk area because it can commit syntactically or semantically invalid code after naive marker deletion.
Metaswarm commander branches are outside the normal merger/cleanup path.

priority_fixes.fix: Add post-conflict marker validation and available syntax/test validation before committing auto-resolved conflicts.
Detect BASE_BRANCH once and use it consistently for checkout, pull, diff sizing, and merge-base comparisons.
Add clean-working-tree guard and warning/confirmation flag around destructive merged-branch reset.
Deduplicate branch discovery and optionally add stampede/* fallback for missing result JSONs.
Reconcile README, AGENTS.md, merger-agent docs, and skill docs on branch naming, merge order, and cleanup.
Document or implement cleanup for retained stampede/* and havoc-swarm/* branches.
